questions.question: What are the four pillars of OOP?
questions.answer:
The four pillars are the core principles that define object-oriented programming. I'll explain each with how I apply them in .NET:

  • Encapsulation — bundle data and behavior together, hide internal implementation behind a public interface. In C#: private fields, public properties with getters/setters. The Portfolio class exposes AddHolding() but hides how it validates and adjusts weights internally.
  • Inheritance — a class (child) inherits fields and behaviors from another class (parent). In C#: the : BaseClass syntax. ApplicationUser : IdentityUser inherits all Identity fields while adding custom ones like DisplayName.
  • Polymorphism — the same interface or method behaves differently for different types. In C#: method overriding (virtual/override), method overloading, interfaces.
  • Abstraction — expose only what's necessary, hide complexity. In C#: interfaces and abstract classes define the "what", concrete implementations define the "how". IEmailSender abstracts SMTP vs SendGrid vs console-logger in tests.
followUps: What is the difference between abstraction and encapsulation?
Can you have OOP without inheritance?

followUpAnswers.What is the difference between abstracti:
Abstraction = design-level hiding of complexity (the WHAT). You define what something does via an interface without specifying how. Encapsulation = code-level hiding of internal state (the HOW). Private fields, properties controlling access.

Classic analogy: A car abstracts steering/acceleration (you use pedals and wheel) and encapsulates its engine (you can't reach in directly).

  • In C#: IEmailSender is abstraction.
  • private readonly string _smtpHost is encapsulation.
followUpAnswers.Can you have OOP without inheritance?:
Yes — interfaces, composition, and polymorphism are sufficient. Go (the language) has no class inheritance at all — it uses interfaces and struct embedding.

Modern .NET design strongly favors this: DI containers work entirely through interfaces with no inheritance required. Composition assembles behavior from smaller objects; interfaces define contracts.

Inheritance is one tool among many, not the foundation of OOP.

questions.tip: Abstraction vs Encapsulation: Abstraction is about hiding complexity at the design level ("what it does"). Encapsulation is about hiding implementation at the code level ("how it does it"). Interfaces are abstraction; private fields are encapsulation.

questions.question: What is the difference between an interface and an abstract class?
questions.answer:
Both define a contract that implementing classes must fulfill, but they have key differences:

FeatureInterfaceAbstract Class
InstantiationCannot instantiateCannot instantiate
Multiple inheritanceClass can implement manyClass can extend only one
State (fields)No instance fields (C# 8: static fields only)Can have instance fields
Default implementationsYes (C# 8+)Yes (non-abstract methods)
ConstructorNoYes (called by derived class)

When I use what:

  • Interface — for defining capabilities that unrelated classes can share. IEmailSender, IProgressService — any class can implement it regardless of its hierarchy. Perfect for DI and mocking.
  • Abstract class — for sharing code among closely related classes. BasePaymentProcessor with shared validation logic, leaving only ProcessPaymentAsync() abstract for subclasses to implement.

In the CSInvoice project, IDocumentGenerate is an interface because unrelated invoice types (Normal, CP, Monthly) all implement the same contract but share no common code.
followUps: When would you choose abstract class over interface?
What are default interface methods in C# 8?

followUpAnswers.When would you choose abstract class ove:
Choose abstract class when:

  • You need to share implementation code among closely related classes (Template Method pattern — base class defines the algorithm steps, derived classes fill in specifics).
  • You need non-public members (protected fields, protected methods) that derived classes share.
  • You need a constructor to initialize shared state.
  • You need a true IS-A relationship with a common ancestor.

Example: BaseDocumentGenerator with shared ValidateInput(), LogGeneration() methods, and only GenerateContent() abstract for each document type to implement.

In contrast, interfaces are preferred for defining capabilities that unrelated classes can share (IDisposable, IComparable, IEmailSender).
followUpAnswers.What are default interface methods in C#:
C# 8 added the ability to add method implementations directly inside interfaces. An implementing class does not need to provide that method — it gets the default.

Primary use case: evolving a library interface without breaking existing implementors (add a new method with a default implementation; old classes continue to compile).

Downsides:

  • Can lead to diamond problem-like ambiguity if two interfaces provide conflicting defaults.
  • Surprising that a class can have behavior it never wrote.

Best practice: use sparingly — prefer abstract base classes when shared implementation is the intent.

questions.tip: Prefer interfaces over abstract classes for .NET dependency injection — you can only mock/substitute interfaces cleanly. Abstract classes are fine for template method pattern where you're sharing implementation, not just a contract.

questions.question: What is polymorphism and what are its types in C#?
questions.answer:
Polymorphism means "many forms" — the ability to treat different types through a common interface. Two main types in C#:

1. Compile-time (static) polymorphism — method overloading:

public class EmailSender
{
    public void Send(string to, string subject) { ... }
    public void Send(string to, string subject, string template) { ... }
    // Same name, different parameter signatures — resolved at compile time
}

2. Runtime (dynamic) polymorphism — method overriding via virtual/override:

public abstract class NotificationSender
{
    public abstract Task SendAsync(Notification n);
}

public class EmailNotificationSender : NotificationSender
{
    public override async Task SendAsync(Notification n) { /* SMTP logic */ }
}

public class SmsNotificationSender : NotificationSender
{
    public override async Task SendAsync(Notification n) { /* SMS logic */ }
}

// Polymorphic usage — correct Send() called at runtime
NotificationSender sender = GetSender(notificationType);
await sender.SendAsync(notification);
followUps: What is method hiding vs method overriding?
What is operator overloading?

followUpAnswers.What is method hiding vs method overridi:
With virtual/override (true overriding): the runtime uses the derived class method even when the variable is typed as the base class — this is virtual dispatch / polymorphism.

With new (hiding): the derived class defines its own method with the same name, but calling through a base-typed variable still calls the BASE method. The new method only activates when the variable is typed as the derived class.

Code example: Animal a = new Dog(); a.Speak()

  • With override: calls Dog.Speak (polymorphism).
  • With new (hiding): calls Animal.Speak.

Always use override for polymorphism; use new only intentionally to deliberately hide without polymorphism (rare).
followUpAnswers.What is operator overloading?:
Defining custom behavior for standard operators (+, -, ==, !=, <, >, [], etc.) on your types. In C# use static methods with the operator keyword.

Classic use cases: Money + Money, Vector2 + Vector2, Complex numbers.

public static Money operator +(Money a, Money b)
    => new Money(a.Amount + b.Amount, a.Currency);

C# 9+ records get value-based == and != automatically. Comparison operators should be defined consistently (implement IComparable). Equality operators should also override GetHashCode and Equals.

questions.tip: Method hiding vs overriding: override replaces the base method (virtual dispatch). new hides the base method — calling through the base type still calls the base method. Always prefer override; use new only intentionally.

questions.question: What is encapsulation and how do you implement it in C#?
questions.answer:
Encapsulation means bundling data and the methods that operate on it, and restricting direct access to internal state. The class exposes a clean public API while hiding implementation details.

C# access modifiers:

  • public — accessible from anywhere
  • private — accessible only within the class
  • protected — accessible within class and derived classes
  • internal — accessible within the same assembly
  • private protected — derived classes in same assembly only
public class Portfolio
{
    private decimal _totalValue;
    private readonly List<Holding> _holdings = [];

    public decimal TotalValue => _totalValue;  // read-only exposure
    public IReadOnlyList<Holding> Holdings => _holdings.AsReadOnly();

    public void AddHolding(Holding holding)
    {
        ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(holding);
        if (holding.Quantity <= 0)
            throw new ArgumentException("Quantity must be positive");
        _holdings.Add(holding);
        RecalculateTotalValue();
    }

    private void RecalculateTotalValue()  // internal implementation hidden
    {
        _totalValue = _holdings.Sum(h => h.Quantity * h.CurrentPrice);
    }
}
followUps: What is the difference between a field and a property?
What is a readonly field vs a const?

followUpAnswers.What is the difference between a field a:
A field is a direct storage variable in a class (e.g., private int _count). A property is a controlled access mechanism with get/set accessors that can contain logic (validation, lazy loading, change notification).

Properties should be the public face of your class — they allow you to add logic later without breaking callers. Fields should always be private. Properties can be auto-implemented (public string Name { get; set; }) when no logic is needed.

Key differences:

  • Properties appear in serialization, data binding, and reflection in ways fields don't.
  • Properties can be defined in interfaces.
  • Properties can have different access modifiers for get vs set (public string Name { get; private set; }).
followUpAnswers.What is a readonly field vs a const?:
readonly: set once — either in the declaration or in the constructor. Can be different per instance. Can be any type. Value determined at runtime.

const: compile-time literal only. Must be a primitive type. Same value for all instances and is baked into the calling assembly at compile time — changing a const in a library requires recompiling all callers.

Guidance:

  • Use readonly when the value is determined at runtime or construction time, or might change between library versions.
  • Use const only for true never-changing literals (MaxRetries = 3, separator characters).

questions.tip: Expose collections as IReadOnlyList<T> — returning the internal List<T> directly lets callers add/remove items bypassing your validation. Return AsReadOnly() or the IReadOnlyList<T> interface.

questions.question: What is inheritance in C# and what are its limitations?
questions.answer:
Inheritance lets a derived class acquire the members (fields, properties, methods) of a base class and extend or override them.

public class Animal
{
    public string Name { get; set; } = string.Empty;
    public virtual string Speak() => "...";
}

public class Dog : Animal
{
    public override string Speak() => "Woof!";
}

public sealed class Labrador : Dog  // sealed: cannot be further derived
{
    public override string Speak() => "Woof woof!";
}

Limitations of inheritance:

  • Single inheritance only — C# classes can only extend one class (use interfaces for multiple contracts).
  • Tight coupling — changes to the base class ripple to all derived classes. Fragile base class problem.
  • Can't inherit from sealed class — sealed prevents further derivation (e.g., String is sealed).
  • Inheritance is overused — "Favour composition over inheritance" is a core design principle.
followUps: What is the fragile base class problem?
Composition vs inheritance — when to use which?

followUpAnswers.What is the fragile base class problem?:
When modifications to a base class unintentionally break derived classes.

Example: a base class has a virtual method A() that internally calls B(). A derived class overrides B(). When you change A() in the base to also call C(), the derived class's B() override now gets called from both A() and C() — breaking an invariant the derived class author assumed.

The derived class author cannot know what the base class will do with their override. This coupling makes large inheritance hierarchies brittle.

Solution: prefer sealed classes, composition, and thin well-defined base classes. Mark members sealed override to end the chain.
followUpAnswers.Composition vs inheritance — when to use:
The test: IS-A vs HAS-A. Dog IS AN Animal → inheritance valid. Car HAS AN Engine → composition.

Prefer composition because:

  • Behavior can be changed at runtime by swapping composed objects (Strategy pattern).
  • Avoids the fragile base class problem.
  • Easier to test — inject mock components.
  • Avoids explosion of class hierarchy when you have multiple dimensions of variation.

Inherit when: there's a genuine type relationship, you need polymorphism through a base type, and the base class is stable.

In .NET, IdentityUser uses inheritance (ApplicationUser : IdentityUser) where the IS-A relationship is genuine.

questions.tip: Composition over Inheritance rule of thumb: Ask "is a" vs "has a". Dog IS AN Animal → inheritance. Car HAS AN Engine → composition. If in doubt, compose — it's easier to change later.

questions.question: What are generics in C# and why are they useful?
questions.answer:
Generics allow you to write type-safe, reusable code without specifying the actual type until the code is used. The compiler enforces type safety at compile time (no boxing/unboxing, no casting).

// Generic repository — works for any entity type
public interface IRepository<T> where T : class
{
    Task<T?> GetByIdAsync(int id);
    Task<IReadOnlyList<T>> GetAllAsync();
    Task AddAsync(T entity);
}

// Generic result wrapper — avoids exception-driven flow
public class Result<T>
{
    public bool IsSuccess { get; private init; }
    public T? Value { get; private init; }
    public string? Error { get; private init; }

    public static Result<T> Success(T value) =>
        new() { IsSuccess = true, Value = value };

    public static Result<T> Failure(string error) =>
        new() { IsSuccess = false, Error = error };
}

// Generic constraints
public class Repository<T> where T : class, IEntity, new() { ... }

Generic constraints: where T : class (reference type), where T : struct (value type), where T : new() (parameterless constructor), where T : IEntity (must implement interface).
followUps: What is a generic constraint?
What is the difference between covariance and contravariance?

followUpAnswers.What is a generic constraint?:
Constraints limit what types can be used as the generic type parameter and unlock capabilities on the type within the generic code. Written with where T : constraint.

Common constraints:

  • where T : class — reference type only.
  • where T : struct — value type only.
  • where T : new() — must have parameterless constructor (allows new T()).
  • where T : SomeInterface — must implement interface (allows calling that interface's methods on T).
  • where T : SomeBaseClass — must derive from class.
  • where T : notnull — non-nullable, C# 8+.

Multiple constraints: where T : class, IEntity, new().

Without constraints, you can only use object-level operations on T. With where T : IEntity you can call IEntity's methods.
followUpAnswers.What is the difference between covarianc:
These describe how generic types relate when their type parameters have an inheritance relationship.

Covariance (out T): if Dog derives from Animal, then IEnumerable<Dog> can be used where IEnumerable<Animal> is expected. The type parameter appears in output (return) position only.

Contravariance (in T): Action<Animal> can be used where Action<Dog> is expected — a method accepting any Animal can certainly handle a Dog. The type parameter appears in input (parameter) position only.

Invariance: List<Dog> is NOT a List<Animal> — because you could add a Cat through the Animal reference, corrupting the Dog list.

Arrays in C# are covariant (a bug — allows runtime exceptions). Most developers encounter this when assigning IEnumerable<DerivedType> to IEnumerable<BaseType>.

questions.tip: Covariance (out T) — can use a more derived type. IEnumerable<Dog> can be assigned to IEnumerable<Animal>. Contravariance (in T) — can use a less derived type. Used on method parameters. Most developers just need to know generic collections are covariant when the type parameter is out.

questions.question: What are delegates and events in C#?
questions.answer:
A delegate is a type-safe function pointer — a reference to a method that matches a specific signature. Action, Func<T>, and Predicate<T> are built-in delegates.

// Built-in delegates
Func<int, bool> isEven = x => x % 2 == 0;
Action<string> log = Console.WriteLine;

// LINQ uses delegates extensively
var activeItems = items.Where(p => p.IsActive);  // Func<T, bool>

// Event (restricts direct invocation — only class can raise it)
public class PaymentProcessor
{
    public event EventHandler<PaymentCompletedEventArgs>? PaymentCompleted;

    protected virtual void OnPaymentCompleted(PaymentCompletedEventArgs e)
        => PaymentCompleted?.Invoke(this, e);
}

// Subscriber
paymentProcessor.PaymentCompleted += async (sender, e) =>
    await SendReceiptEmailAsync(e.Invoice);

An event wraps a delegate with add/remove accessors — only the declaring class can raise it. Implements the Observer pattern.
followUps: What is the difference between event and delegate?
What is EventHandler<T>?
What are lambda expressions?

followUpAnswers.What is the difference between event and:
A delegate is a type-safe function pointer — anyone with a reference to a delegate field can invoke it directly.

An event wraps a delegate with add/remove accessors — external callers can only subscribe (+=) or unsubscribe (-=), but cannot invoke the event or replace all subscribers. Only the declaring class can raise (invoke) the event.

This encapsulation is essential for the Observer pattern — the publisher controls when events fire; subscribers can only listen.

Public delegate fields are an anti-pattern because any code can invoke them or set them to null, breaking other subscribers.
followUpAnswers.What is EventHandler<T>?:
EventHandler<TEventArgs> is the standard .NET delegate signature for events: void (object sender, TEventArgs e).

  • The sender is the object that raised the event (allows the handler to access the publisher).
  • TEventArgs inherits from EventArgs and carries event-specific data.

Following this convention allows generic event handling infrastructure (event aggregators, logging proxies) to work with any event. Always use this signature for public events — custom delegate signatures are rarely needed.

For events with no data, use the non-generic EventHandler.
followUpAnswers.What are lambda expressions?:
An anonymous inline function defined with the => operator. Used everywhere: LINQ queries (.Where(p => p.IsActive)), event handlers, callbacks, Task.Run(() => DoWork()).

They create closures when they reference variables from the enclosing scope.

See the dedicated lambda question (oops-017) for full details on Func/Action and closure gotchas.

questions.tip: Events vs delegates: A public delegate field lets anyone invoke it. An event wraps the delegate and restricts external code to += and -= only — the raising is encapsulated inside the class. Always use events for the observer pattern.

questions.question: What is the difference between value types and reference types in C#?
questions.answer:
Value types — stored directly where declared (stack for locals). Copying creates an independent copy. Includes: int, double, bool, DateTime, struct, enum.

Reference types — variable holds a reference (pointer) to heap-allocated data. Copying copies the reference, not the data. Includes: class, string, array, interface.

// Value type: copy semantics
DateTime d1 = DateTime.Now;
DateTime d2 = d1;  // independent copy
d2 = d2.AddDays(1);  // d1 unchanged

// Reference type: shared reference
var list1 = new List<int> { 1, 2, 3 };
var list2 = list1;  // both point to same list!
list2.Add(4);  // list1 also has 4!

// String is reference type but behaves like value type (immutable)
string s1 = "hello";
string s2 = s1;
s2 += " world";  // creates a new string, s1 unchanged

// Boxing: value type → object (heap allocation)
object boxed = 42;        // boxing
int unboxed = (int)boxed; // unboxing — expensive, avoid in hot paths
followUps: What is a nullable value type?
What are C# records?
What is struct vs class performance trade-off?

followUpAnswers.What is a nullable value type?:
Value types (int, bool, DateTime, struct) cannot normally be null. Nullable<T> (written as T?) wraps a value type and adds a boolean HasValue flag plus the Value property.

int? i = null;
if (i.HasValue) Use(i.Value);
int count = i ?? 0; // null-coalescing

C# 8 Nullable Reference Types (NRTs): enabling <Nullable>enable</Nullable> in .csproj makes the compiler warn when reference types might be null — string is non-nullable, string? is nullable. NRTs are a static analysis feature, not a runtime change.
followUpAnswers.What are C# records?:
Records (C# 9) are a special class with value-based equality: two record instances are equal if all their properties are equal, regardless of reference.

Features:

  • Auto-generated ==, !=, Equals, GetHashCode, and ToString based on properties.
  • Immutable by default (init-only properties via positional syntax).
  • Non-destructive mutation via with expressions (var p2 = p1 with { X = 5 }).
public record Point(int X, int Y);
var p2 = p1 with { X = 5 }; // new record, p1 unchanged

Perfect for DTOs, domain value objects, and immutable data transfer. record struct gives value semantics with stack allocation.
followUpAnswers.What is struct vs class performance trad:
Struct advantages: stack-allocated (no heap allocation, no GC pressure), value semantics (safe to copy, no reference aliasing bugs), better cache locality in arrays.

Struct disadvantages: copying by value — passing a large struct copies every field (use in parameter for readonly reference). No inheritance. Boxing when assigned to object/interface variable.

Guidelines: use struct for small (≤ 16 bytes), immutable, frequently created short-lived types (Point, Vector2, Color). Use class for anything larger, mutable, or needing inheritance. Mutable structs cause confusing copy-mutation bugs.

questions.tip: Structs are copied by value — passing a large struct to a method copies every field. Use in parameter modifier to pass readonly reference: void Process(in LargeStruct s). Or just use class if it's large or mutable.

questions.question: What is CLR and how does .NET code execute?
questions.answer:
CLR (Common Language Runtime) is the virtual machine that executes .NET code. It provides: memory management (GC), type safety, exception handling, thread management, and security. When you write C#, the compiler produces IL (Intermediate Language) — not native machine code. The CLR then converts IL to native code at runtime via the JIT (Just-In-Time) compiler.

Execution flow:

  1. You write C# → C# compiler produces IL (in .dll/.exe)
  2. Runtime: CLR's JIT compiler converts IL to native machine code on first execution of a method
  3. Native code is cached in memory — subsequent calls run native code directly (no re-JIT)
  4. GC automatically manages object lifetimes

Key CLR services:

  • Garbage Collector — automatic memory management, generational (Gen0/Gen1/Gen2/LOH)
  • Type safety verifier — prevents illegal memory access
  • Exception handling — structured try/catch/finally across all .NET languages
  • Thread support — ThreadPool, Tasks, async/await plumbing

In .NET 8, the ReadyToRun (R2R) compilation model pre-compiles IL to native code at publish time, reducing JIT startup overhead — important for containerized microservices with cold starts.
followUps: What is Native AOT in .NET 8?
What is Roslyn?
What is the difference between .NET CLR and Java JVM?

followUpAnswers.What is Native AOT in .NET 8?:
Ahead-of-Time (AOT) compilation compiles the entire application to native machine code at build time — there is no JIT at runtime.

Benefits:

  • Millisecond startup times (vs ~100ms for JIT-warmed apps).
  • Significantly smaller memory footprint.
  • Single self-contained native binary.
  • No runtime code generation overhead.

Limitations:

  • No Reflection.Emit or runtime code generation.
  • Limited dynamic reflection.
  • Larger binary size than IL-only.
  • Not all NuGet libraries are AOT-compatible.

Best suited for: serverless/AWS Lambda (cold start sensitivity), CLI tools, microservices in containers. In .NET 8, ASP.NET Core supports Native AOT for minimal API scenarios.
followUpAnswers.What is Roslyn?:
Roslyn is the open-source .NET compiler platform — the C# and VB.NET compiler rewritten as a set of APIs (compiler-as-a-service).

Unlike traditional compilers that are black boxes, Roslyn exposes its internal data structures: syntax trees, semantic models (type information, symbol resolution), and diagnostics.

This powers:

  • All Visual Studio / Rider IntelliSense and refactoring.
  • Roslyn Analyzers (custom compile-time rules, like nullable warnings, security checks).
  • Source Generators (code generated at compile time — eliminates runtime reflection for JSON serialization, DI, regex).

If you've used [JsonSerializable] or EF Core compiled models, you've used Roslyn source generators.
followUpAnswers.What is the difference between .NET CLR:
Both are managed runtime environments with GC and JIT compilation, but key differences:

  1. Value types: CLR has true value types (struct) that live on the stack without boxing; Java boxes all primitives for collections.
  2. Generics: CLR has reified generics — List<int> and List<string> are different types at runtime; Java uses type erasure — all List types are the same at runtime (generic info lost).
  3. Language ecosystem: CLR has a unified type system across C#, F#, VB.NET; JVM languages (Kotlin, Scala) compile to bytecode but have their own type systems.
  4. Unsafe code: CLR supports pointers, unsafe blocks, Span<T>, and native interop directly; Java has JNI which is more cumbersome.

questions.tip: Native AOT (.NET 7+) compiles the entire application to native machine code at build time — no JIT at runtime. Startup time drops from ~100ms to single-digit ms, memory footprint shrinks. Trade-off: no runtime code generation (no Reflection.Emit), larger binary.

questions.question: How does Garbage Collection work in .NET? What are generations?
questions.answer:
Garbage Collection (GC) is the CLR's automatic memory manager. It reclaims memory occupied by unreachable objects (objects with no live references) so you don't have to manually free memory.

Generational GC — the key concept:

  • Gen0 — short-lived objects (local variables, temporary allocations). Collected most frequently. Fast — typically takes <1ms.
  • Gen1 — objects that survived Gen0 collection. Buffer between Gen0 and Gen2.
  • Gen2 — long-lived objects (static fields, large caches, DbContext). Collected rarely. Expensive — can take 100ms+.
  • LOH (Large Object Heap) — objects >85KB allocated directly here. Only collected during Gen2 (full GC).
// Prefer object pooling for frequently allocated large objects
var pool = new ObjectPool<LargeBuffer>(() => new LargeBuffer());
var buffer = pool.Get();
try {
    // use buffer
} finally {
    pool.Return(buffer); // reuse instead of GC
}

// Dispose patterns trigger GC-eligible state
public class DatabaseContext : IDisposable
{
    private bool _disposed;

    public void Dispose()
    {
        if (!_disposed) {
            _connection?.Dispose();
            _disposed = true;
        }
        GC.SuppressFinalize(this);  // don't need finalizer now
    }
}

In FolioTrack, I kept FundHoldingsService as a Singleton and cached fund data in-memory (Gen2) — intentional, infrequently updated. Short-lived DTO projections stay in Gen0 and are collected quickly.
followUps: What is IDisposable and when should you implement it?
What is a finalizer and when is it called?
How do you diagnose a memory leak in .NET?

followUpAnswers.What is IDisposable and when should you:
Implement IDisposable when your class: (1) directly wraps an unmanaged resource (file handle, native socket, Win32 handle via P/Invoke); (2) holds one or more IDisposable fields (e.g., a service that owns a SqlConnection or StreamReader).

The Dispose() method is called deterministically — either explicitly or via the using statement — to release resources immediately without waiting for GC.

Don't implement IDisposable speculatively — only when you own resources. In ASP.NET Core DI, Scoped and Transient services that implement IDisposable are automatically disposed at end of request.
followUpAnswers.What is a finalizer and when is it calle:
A finalizer (~ClassName()) is called by the GC's finalizer thread when the object is being collected — at a non-deterministic time after it becomes unreachable. Used as a last-resort safety net: if Dispose() was never called, the finalizer can still release unmanaged resources.

Problems: non-deterministic timing; runs on GC finalizer thread (no async); finalizable objects need two GC cycles to collect.

Always call GC.SuppressFinalize(this) at the end of Dispose(). Prefer SafeHandle over finalizers for wrapping OS resources.
followUpAnswers.How do you diagnose a memory leak in .NE:
Tools:

  • dotnet-counters monitor --counters System.Runtime — watch GC heap size, Gen0/1/2 collections in real time.
  • dotnet-dump collect + dotnet-dump analyze — take a process memory snapshot and inspect it.
  • Visual Studio Diagnostic Tools (heap snapshot comparison).
  • Application Insights / Prometheus (long-term GC metrics in production).

Common causes: event handlers subscribed but never unsubscribed; static collections growing unbounded; Singleton holding Scoped services (captive dependency); unmanaged resources not disposed.

Symptom pattern: heap grows steadily over time, Gen2 full collections become frequent, OutOfMemoryException eventually.

questions.tip: GC pressure rule of thumb: Don't fight the GC — it's well-optimized for short-lived objects. Optimize when profiling shows Gen2 collections happening frequently (full GC). Use dotnet-counters or Application Insights to monitor GC stats in production.

questions.question: What is the difference between var and dynamic in C#?
questions.answer:
Both allow you to skip explicit type declarations, but they work completely differently:

Featurevardynamic
Type resolutionCompile time (inferred from RHS)Runtime (late binding)
Type safetyFully type-safeNo compile-time checking
IntelliSenseFull IntelliSenseNo IntelliSense
PerformanceSame as explicit typeSlower (DLR overhead)
// var: compiler infers type at compile time
var name = "Alice";     // string — fully type-safe
var count = items.Where(x => x.Active).Count();  // int
name = 42;              // COMPILER ERROR: can't assign int to string

// dynamic: resolved at runtime — dangerous
dynamic obj = "hello";
obj = 42;               // no error — type changes at runtime!
obj.NonExistentMethod(); // runtime exception: RuntimeBinderException

// Valid use of dynamic: COM interop, reflection simplification
dynamic excel = Activator.CreateInstance(Type.GetTypeFromProgID("Excel.Application"));
excel.Visible = true;  // no need to cast

I use var everywhere for readability. dynamic is rare — only for COM interop or deserializing unknown JSON shapes. In all my projects (FolioTrack, ServiceSelection) I avoid dynamic.
followUps: Can you use var for method parameters?
What is the DLR (Dynamic Language Runtime)?

followUpAnswers.Can you use var for method parameters?:
No — var can only be used for local variable declarations where the right-hand side provides enough information for the compiler to infer the type.

Method parameters, return types, field declarations, and property types all require explicit types. This is intentional: method signatures are contracts consumed by other developers and tools (IntelliSense, documentation, API clients). Making them explicitly typed is essential for readability and discoverability.
followUpAnswers.What is the DLR (Dynamic Language Runtim:
The DLR is a runtime layer that sits on top of the CLR to support dynamic dispatch and dynamic languages. It powers the dynamic keyword in C#.

Key components:

  1. Call site caching — the first time a dynamic call is made, the DLR resolves the method and caches the result; repeated calls with the same types skip re-resolution.
  2. Expression trees — dynamic operations are compiled to expression trees and executed.
  3. Language interop — allows IronPython and IronRuby objects to work with C#'s dynamic keyword.

The DLR is rarely needed directly in modern C# — its main use cases are better served by source generators and System.Text.Json today.

questions.tip: var is NOT the same as dynamic. var is just syntactic sugar — the actual type is known at compile time. dynamic bypasses compile-time type checking entirely. Always prefer var over dynamic unless you specifically need late binding.

questions.question: What is the difference between const, readonly, and static readonly?
questions.answer:
All three create fields that can't be arbitrarily changed, but their timing and scope differ:

Featureconstreadonlystatic readonly
When setCompile timeConstructor or initializerStatic constructor or initializer
Can change per instanceNoYes (in constructor)No (single value)
Runtime value allowedNo (literal only)YesYes
public class Config
{
    // const: baked into calling assembly at compile time
    public const int MaxRetries = 3;  // must be a literal
    public const string DefaultCulture = "en-US";

    // readonly: set once in constructor, can vary per instance
    private readonly string _connectionString;
    public Config(string cs) { _connectionString = cs; }

    // static readonly: set once at class initialization, runtime value OK
    public static readonly DateTime AppStartTime = DateTime.UtcNow;
    public static readonly string Version =
        Assembly.GetExecutingAssembly().GetName().Version!.ToString();
}

Key gotcha with const: If you change a const value in assembly A, assembly B must be recompiled to see the new value — the old value was baked in at compile time. Use static readonly if the value might change between versions.
followUps: What is a readonly struct?
When would you use const vs static readonly?

followUpAnswers.What is a readonly struct?:
A readonly struct guarantees the entire struct is immutable — all fields must be readonly, all properties must be get-only, and all methods implicitly have a this that is a readonly reference.

Benefits: the compiler can pass it by readonly reference without making a defensive copy (critical for performance with in parameters); communicates immutability intent; enables compiler optimizations.

public readonly struct Vector2
{
    public float X { get; }
    public float Y { get; }
    public Vector2(float x, float y) { X = x; Y = y; }
}

C# 8 also added the readonly modifier on individual struct methods to allow partial mutability.
followUpAnswers.When would you use const vs static reado:
Use const for: true compile-time constants that are primitive literals and will NEVER change across library versions (mathematical approximations, separator chars, small integers).

Use static readonly for:

  1. Values computed at startup (static readonly DateTime AppStart = DateTime.UtcNow).
  2. Complex objects that can't be const (arrays, class instances).
  3. Values in a public library that might change between versions — const values are baked into the calling assembly and require recompilation to update.
  4. When in doubt.

The version-baking problem with const is a real gotcha: if library v1 has const int MaxSize = 100 and v2 changes it to 200, callers still see 100 until they recompile.

questions.tip: Use static readonly instead of const for public API values that might change between library versions. const is baked into the calling assembly — a version bump without recompile will use the old value. static readonly is evaluated fresh at runtime.

questions.question: What is the IDisposable pattern and when do you implement it?
questions.answer:
IDisposable is a contract for releasing unmanaged resources (file handles, database connections, network sockets) deterministically — without waiting for the GC. Implement it when your class holds resources that must be freed promptly.

public class ReportGenerator : IDisposable
{
    private Stream? _outputStream;
    private bool _disposed;

    public ReportGenerator(string filePath)
    {
        _outputStream = File.Create(filePath);
    }

    public void GenerateReport() { /* write to stream */ }

    public void Dispose()
    {
        Dispose(disposing: true);
        GC.SuppressFinalize(this); // finalizer not needed
    }

    protected virtual void Dispose(bool disposing)
    {
        if (!_disposed)
        {
            if (disposing)
                _outputStream?.Dispose();
            _disposed = true;
        }
    }
}

// Usage — C# using statement guarantees Dispose() is called
using var generator = new ReportGenerator("report.pdf");
generator.GenerateReport();
// Dispose() called automatically at end of using block

// C# 8+ using declaration (same behavior, less nesting)
using var conn = new SqlConnection(connectionString);

The using statement is syntactic sugar for a try/finally block that calls Dispose(). In all my projects, any class inheriting from EF Core's DbContext, SqlConnection, or HttpClient flows through using blocks.
followUps: What is the difference between Dispose and Finalize?
What does GC.SuppressFinalize do?
What is IAsyncDisposable?

followUpAnswers.What is the difference between Dispose a:
Dispose(): called deterministically by your code (or via using), runs on the calling thread, you control exactly when it happens.

Finalize() (~ClassName()): called non-deterministically by the GC when collecting the object, runs on the GC finalizer thread, no control over timing.

The standard IDisposable pattern implements BOTH: Dispose() does immediate cleanup and calls GC.SuppressFinalize(this) to skip the finalizer. The finalizer only runs as a safety net if Dispose() was never called.

Never rely solely on the finalizer for cleanup — it may take seconds or minutes after the object is unreachable.
followUpAnswers.What does GC.SuppressFinalize do?:
It removes the object from the GC's finalization queue, telling the runtime: "this object's finalizer doesn't need to run — cleanup is already done."

When Dispose() is called correctly, all resources are released immediately — there's no need to run the finalizer later. Calling GC.SuppressFinalize(this) in Dispose() saves the GC from having to track the object through an extra collection cycle. This improves both throughput and memory efficiency.

Always call it as the last line of Dispose().
followUpAnswers.What is IAsyncDisposable?:
Introduced in .NET Core 3.0, IAsyncDisposable defines ValueTask DisposeAsync() for async-friendly cleanup. Used when releasing resources requires async operations: gracefully closing a database connection, flushing an async stream buffer, waiting for in-flight work to complete.

Used with await using syntax:

await using var conn = new AsyncDbConnection();

The compiler generates an async try/finally that calls DisposeAsync(). EF Core's DbContext, IAsyncEnumerator<T>, and many ASP.NET Core components implement IAsyncDisposable. Prefer it over Dispose() in async code paths to avoid blocking.

questions.tip: In ASP.NET Core DI, registered services are automatically disposed at the end of the request (Scoped) or app shutdown (Singleton). You still need using for objects created manually (new SqlConnection, new StreamReader, etc.).

questions.question: What is the difference between string and StringBuilder in C#?
questions.answer:
string is immutable — every concatenation or modification creates a new string object on the heap. In a loop, this generates enormous GC pressure.

StringBuilder is mutable — it maintains an internal buffer and appends without creating new objects each time.

// BAD: creates 1000 string objects
string result = "";
for (int i = 0; i < 1000; i++)
    result += i.ToString();  // each += allocates a new string!

// GOOD: one StringBuilder, minimal allocations
var sb = new StringBuilder(capacity: 5000); // pre-size for efficiency
for (int i = 0; i < 1000; i++)
    sb.Append(i);
string result = sb.ToString();  // single string created at end

// Real-world example: email body builder in PaymentReminder service
var emailBody = new StringBuilder();
emailBody.AppendLine("Dear ").Append(client.Name).AppendLine(",");
emailBody.AppendLine($"Your invoice #{inv.Id} of {inv.Amount:C} is due.");
emailBody.AppendLine("Regards, Billing Team");
string body = emailBody.ToString();

When to use what:

  • string — concatenating 2-3 strings, string literals, simple operations
  • StringBuilder — concatenating in loops, building large strings, HTML/email body generation
  • string.Join() — joining a known collection — fastest option, internally uses efficient buffer
followUps: What is string interning?
What is Span<char> for string operations?

followUpAnswers.What is string interning?:
The CLR maintains a pool of unique string literals called the intern pool. When two string literals with the same value appear in code, they share the same memory address — reference equality holds.

  • string.Intern(s) adds a string to the pool and returns the canonical reference.
  • string.IsInterned(s) returns the interned reference if present, or null.

Benefits: memory savings for frequently repeated strings; reference equality checks become pointer comparisons (fast).

Drawbacks: interned strings are never garbage collected until the process exits — don't intern large or rarely-repeated strings.

Practical use: configuration keys, HTTP header names, frequently compared identifiers. All string literals in your code are automatically interned by the CLR.
followUpAnswers.What is Span<char> for string operations:
Span<char> is a stack-allocated view (a slice) over a contiguous memory region — it can view part of a string, array, or stack memory without allocating a copy.

For string operations: instead of s.Substring(3, 5) which allocates a new string, use s.AsSpan(3, 5) which is zero-allocation — it's just a pointer + length into the original string.

Critical for high-performance parsing (HTTP request parsers, CSV readers) where substring creation in hot paths causes GC pressure.

ReadOnlySpan<char> is the immutable version for strings.

Limitation: Span<T> is a ref struct — it can only live on the stack, cannot be stored in class fields. For async scenarios or field storage, use Memory<char> instead (heap-compatible view).

questions.tip: string.Join is often faster than StringBuilder for collections: string.Join(", ", items) is cleaner and optimized. Use StringBuilder when you're building a string step-by-step with conditional logic.

questions.question: What is a sealed class and when would you use it?
questions.answer:
A sealed class cannot be inherited — it terminates the inheritance chain. A sealed method in an unsealed class prevents further overriding in derived classes.

// Sealed class — no further inheritance allowed
public sealed class JwtHelper
{
    private readonly string _secret;
    public JwtHelper(string secret) { _secret = secret; }

    public string GenerateToken(int userId, string email)
    {
        // JWT generation logic
    }
}

// Cannot extend: public class ExtendedJwtHelper : JwtHelper {} // COMPILE ERROR

// Sealed method — prevent overriding in further derived classes
public class PaymentBase
{
    protected virtual void Validate() { ... }
}

public class CreditCardPayment : PaymentBase
{
    public sealed override void Validate() { ... } // cannot be overridden further
}

When to use sealed:

  • Classes designed as utilities with no extension points (JwtHelper, static-like classes)
  • Performance-critical classes — JIT can devirtualize calls on sealed classes (inlining opportunity)
  • Security — prevent substitution attacks via inheritance
  • Value types (structs) are implicitly sealed
followUps: Does sealing a class improve performance?
Why is string sealed in .NET?

followUpAnswers.Does sealing a class improve performance:
Yes, measurably in hot paths. When calling a virtual method on a sealed type, the JIT compiler can devirtualize the call — it knows exactly which method implementation will be called, so it can inline it rather than performing an indirect vtable lookup.

Devirtualization eliminates the branch, enables further inlining optimizations, and improves CPU branch prediction. Benchmarks show 5–15% improvement for tight loops over sealed types with many virtual calls.

However, for typical application code (controllers, services, repositories), this is negligible — the bottleneck is I/O, not virtual dispatch.

Seal classes for correctness (design intent, preventing unsafe subclassing), not as a primary performance optimization. The performance benefit is a welcome side effect.
followUpAnswers.Why is string sealed in .NET?:
Multiple reasons:

  1. Security — if String were extensible, a malicious subclass could override GetHashCode() or Equals() to break Dictionary lookups, hash collisions, or security equality checks. String comparisons underpin many security-sensitive operations.
  2. Interning and identity guarantees — the CLR has special knowledge of String's memory layout for string interning; subclasses would complicate this.
  3. Runtime optimizations — the JIT has special fast paths for string operations that rely on knowing the exact layout.
  4. Immutability guarantee — if you could subclass string and add mutable state, the immutability contract breaks.

The sealed + immutable combination is what makes strings safe to share across threads and use as dictionary keys.

questions.tip: String is sealed for safety: If string were extensible, an attacker could create a malicious subclass with a modified GetHashCode() or Equals() — breaking Dictionary lookups and security checks. Many framework types are sealed for the same reason.

questions.question: What is exception handling in C#? What is the difference between throw and throw ex?
questions.answer:
Exception handling in C# uses try-catch-finally blocks. The try block contains code that might throw. The catch block handles specific exception types. The finally block runs regardless of whether an exception occurred — used for cleanup.

// try-catch-finally pattern
public async Task<Invoice> GenerateInvoiceAsync(int assignmentId)
{
    SqlConnection? conn = null;
    try
    {
        conn = new SqlConnection(_connectionString);
        await conn.OpenAsync();
        // ... invoice generation
        return invoice;
    }
    catch (SqlException ex) when (ex.Number == 1205)  // deadlock
    {
        _logger.LogWarning("Deadlock on invoice {Id}, retrying", assignmentId);
        throw;  // rethrow — preserve stack trace
    }
    catch (Exception ex)
    {
        _logger.LogError(ex, "Invoice generation failed for {Id}", assignmentId);
        throw;  // CORRECT: throw preserves original stack trace
        // throw ex; // WRONG: resets stack trace to this line
    }
    finally
    {
        conn?.Dispose(); // always runs
    }
}

throw vs throw ex:

  • throw — rethrows the exception preserving the original stack trace (where it actually happened)
  • throw ex — resets the stack trace to the current line — you lose the original call stack, making debugging much harder

Exception filters (catch (Exception ex) when (condition)) — the filter runs before the stack unwinds, preserving the full stack trace while allowing conditional handling.
followUps: What is AggregateException?
What is custom exception creation?
What is exception filter?

followUpAnswers.What is AggregateException?:
AggregateException wraps multiple exceptions into one. It's thrown by the Task Parallel Library (TPL) when multiple parallel operations fail simultaneously — Task.WaitAll(tasks) or Parallel.ForEach() collect all exceptions and wrap them in a single AggregateException.

Each individual exception is in the InnerExceptions collection.

  • When you await a failed task, C# automatically unwraps the first inner exception (you see the original exception, not the aggregate).
  • When you use .Wait() or .Result (blocking access), you get the raw AggregateException.
catch (AggregateException ae)
{
    ae.Handle(ex =>
    {
        if (ex is OperationCanceledException) return true;
        _logger.LogError(ex, "Task failed");
        return false;
    });
}
followUpAnswers.What is custom exception creation?:
Create a class inheriting from Exception (or a specific subclass matching your scenario). Add properties for domain-specific context. Always provide the standard constructor overloads (message, message+inner).

public class InvoiceNotFoundException : Exception
{
    public int InvoiceId { get; }

    public InvoiceNotFoundException(int id)
        : base($"Invoice {id} not found")
    {
        InvoiceId = id;
    }

    public InvoiceNotFoundException(int id, Exception inner)
        : base($"Invoice {id} not found", inner)
    {
        InvoiceId = id;
    }
}

Benefits: catch-site can filter by specific exception type (catch (InvoiceNotFoundException ex)), structured logging captures the InvoiceId, meaningful to callers without parsing message strings.
followUpAnswers.What is exception filter?:
catch (Exception ex) when (condition) — the when clause evaluates BEFORE the stack unwinds. If the condition is false, the catch block is bypassed as if it weren't there, and the exception propagates to the next handler up the call stack.

Key benefits:

  1. You can inspect the exception and decide conditionally whether to handle it — without losing the original stack trace.
  2. The debugger stops at the throw site, not the catch site, giving you full context.
  3. More expressive than catching and re-throwing.

Example: catch (SqlException ex) when (ex.Number == 1205) handles only SQL deadlock errors. Compare with catching-and-rethrowing which unwinds the stack first (losing the original frame in debugger).

questions.tip: Always use throw not throw ex when rethrowing. The stack trace is your most valuable debugging tool in production. Losing it with throw ex forces you to reproduce the bug rather than reading the trace.

questions.question: What are lambda expressions and how are they used in C#?
questions.answer:
A lambda expression is an anonymous function — a short inline function that can be assigned to a delegate or passed as a method argument. Syntax: parameters => expression.

// Basic lambda
Func<int, int> square = x => x * x;
Console.WriteLine(square(5)); // 25

// Multi-parameter
Func<int, int, int> add = (a, b) => a + b;

// Statement lambda (block body)
Func<int, bool> isPrime = n => {
    if (n < 2) return false;
    for (int i = 2; i <= Math.Sqrt(n); i++)
        if (n % i == 0) return false;
    return true;
};

// In LINQ (most common usage)
var activePortfolios = portfolios
    .Where(p => p.IsActive)                     // filter
    .Select(p => new { p.Id, p.Name })           // project
    .OrderByDescending(p => p.CreatedAt)          // sort
    .ToList();

// In event handlers
btnSave.Click += async (sender, e) => await SaveAsync();

// Capturing variables (closure)
string prefix = "Hello";
Action<string> greet = name => Console.WriteLine($"{prefix}, {name}!"); // captures prefix
greet("Alice"); // "Hello, Alice!"

// C# 9+ shorter lambda for property access
var names = people.Select(p => p.Name).ToList(); // common pattern

Lambdas are the backbone of LINQ and make collection operations concise. I use them throughout FolioTrack's service layer for filtering and projecting EF Core queries.
followUps: What is the difference between Func and Action?
What is a closure and what are the capture gotchas?

followUpAnswers.What is the difference between Func and:
Both are built-in generic delegate types.

Action: represents a method that returns void. Action<T> takes one parameter, up to Action<T1..T16>. Used for side-effecting operations: Action<string> log = Console.WriteLine;

Func<TResult>: represents a method that returns a value. The last type parameter is always the return type. Up to 16 input parameters: Func<T1..T16, TResult>. Used for transformations and predicates: Func<int, bool> isEven = x => x % 2 == 0;

Predicate<T> is shorthand for Func<T, bool> — used in List.FindAll, Array.Exists.

Rule of thumb: use Func when you need a return value, Action when you don't.
followUpAnswers.What is a closure and what are the captu:
A closure is when a lambda captures (closes over) variables from its enclosing scope. The lambda holds a REFERENCE to the variable, not a copy of its value at the moment of capture. So if the variable changes later, the lambda sees the new value.

Classic gotcha in for loops:

for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++)
{
    list.Add(() => Console.WriteLine(i));
}
// All 5 lambdas print 5 — they all reference the same i variable,
// which is 5 after the loop ends.

Fix: create a local copy inside the loop:

var captured = i;
list.Add(() => Console.WriteLine(captured));
// Each lambda captures a different captured variable.

C# foreach loops don't have this bug (each iteration creates a new variable). Another gotcha: closures in async lambdas can extend object lifetimes longer than expected, preventing GC.

questions.tip: Closure capture gotcha with loops: Capturing a loop variable in a lambda captures the variable, not its current value. By the time the lambda executes, the loop may have finished and the variable holds the last value. Fix: var captured = loopVar; var lambda = () => Use(captured);
